name: Islamic Theology
title: Allah - Islamic Theology

sources.passage: Surah Al-Ikhlas (112) - Declaration of God's Oneness

extendedContent.title: Origins and Creation in Islamic Theology
extendedContent.content:
Islamic theology posits Allah as the sole creator of the universe, a concept deeply rooted in the Quran. Surah Al-Ikhlas (112:1-4) succinctly encapsulates this: "Say, 'He is Allah, [who is] One, Allah, the Eternal Refuge. He neither begets nor is born, Nor is there to Him any equivalent.'" This establishes Allah's unique position as the origin of all existence, uncreated and without parallel. The Quranic narratives of creation, detailed in Surah Al-A'raf (7:54) and others, describe Allah establishing the heavens and the earth in six days, followed by His ascension to the Throne (Arsh). This creation is not merely a physical act but also an expression of Allah's divine will and wisdom, demonstrating His absolute power and knowledge. 

The concept of 'Kun faya kun' (Be, and it is) further illustrates Allah's creative power. It signifies that Allah's command is immediately fulfilled, highlighting the ease and immediacy with which He brings things into existence. Angels, also created by Allah, play a vital role in the cosmic order, carrying out His commands and acting as intermediaries between Allah and humanity. The story of the creation of Adam, the first human, is central to Islamic theology. Created from clay and given a soul by Allah, Adam is bestowed with knowledge and dominion over the earth, making him Allah's vicegerent (Khalifa). 

The narrative of Iblis (Satan), who refused to bow down to Adam, underscores the importance of submission to Allah's will. Iblis's rebellion stems from pride and arrogance, serving as a cautionary tale against disobedience. While Iblis is granted temporary freedom to tempt humanity, Allah's ultimate power and control remain absolute. The creation narrative, therefore, establishes Allah as the ultimate source of all existence, the sustainer of the universe, and the judge of humankind.

extendedContent.title: Worship and Submission (Ibadah) to Allah
extendedContent.content:
Worship (Ibadah) in Islam is the ultimate expression of submission and devotion to Allah. It encompasses both ritualistic acts and the entirety of one's life lived in accordance with Allah's commands. The five pillars of Islam – Shahada (declaration of faith), Salat (prayer), Zakat (charity), Sawm (fasting), and Hajj (pilgrimage) – form the foundation of obligatory worship. Salat, performed five times daily facing the Kaaba in Mecca, is a direct connection between the believer and Allah, a time for supplication, remembrance, and reflection. Zakat, the giving of a portion of one's wealth to the needy, reflects Allah's attribute of generosity and promotes social justice.

Fasting during Ramadan is a period of spiritual purification and self-restraint, fostering empathy and strengthening one's relationship with Allah. Hajj, the pilgrimage to Mecca, is a journey of spiritual renewal and a demonstration of unity among Muslims from all over the world. Beyond these obligatory acts, worship extends to all aspects of life. Actions performed with the intention of pleasing Allah, such as acts of kindness, honesty, and justice, are considered acts of worship. The concept of 'Dhikr' (remembrance of Allah) is central to Islamic spirituality. This involves constantly being mindful of Allah in all situations, through prayer, contemplation, and recitation of the Quran.

The Quran emphasizes the importance of sincere intention (Niyyah) in worship. Actions performed without genuine devotion are considered worthless. The Quran and Sunnah (the teachings and practices of Prophet Muhammad) provide guidance on how to perform worship correctly and sincerely. Ultimately, the goal of worship is to attain closeness to Allah and to achieve spiritual fulfillment. It is through worship that believers express their gratitude for Allah's blessings, seek His guidance, and strive to live a life that is pleasing to Him.

extendedContent.title: The 99 Names of Allah (Al-Asma al-Husna): Divine Attributes
extendedContent.content:
Islamic theology describes Allah through 99 Beautiful Names (Al-Asma al-Husna), each representing a divine attribute. These names are not merely labels but reflect the essence and qualities of Allah. Understanding and reflecting upon these names is considered a profound form of worship and a path to spiritual growth. Some of the most commonly known names include Ar-Rahman (The Most Compassionate), Ar-Rahim (The Most Merciful), Al-Malik (The Sovereign), Al-Quddus (The Holy), and As-Salam (The Source of Peace). Ar-Rahman and Ar-Rahim, often mentioned together, emphasize Allah's boundless compassion and mercy towards all creation.

Al-Malik highlights Allah's absolute sovereignty and dominion over the universe. Al-Quddus signifies Allah's purity and transcendence, free from any imperfection. As-Salam reflects Allah's role as the source of peace and security. Other significant names include Al-Alim (The All-Knowing), Al-Hakim (The Wise), Al-Qadir (The All-Powerful), and Al-Basir (The All-Seeing). Al-Alim and Al-Hakim emphasize Allah's infinite knowledge and wisdom in creation and governance. Al-Qadir highlights Allah's absolute power and ability to do all things. Al-Basir underscores Allah's awareness of everything that occurs, both seen and unseen.

The Quran and Sunnah encourage Muslims to invoke Allah by His names and to reflect upon their meanings. Doing so helps to deepen one's understanding of Allah and to cultivate a closer relationship with Him. Each name offers a unique perspective on the divine nature, revealing different aspects of Allah's majesty, beauty, and perfection. Contemplating the 99 Names is considered a continuous journey of spiritual discovery, leading to a greater appreciation of Allah's greatness and a stronger connection to the divine.

extendedContent.title: Allah's Role in the Cosmic Order and Divine Decree (Qadar)
extendedContent.content:
In Islamic theology, Allah is not only the creator of the universe but also its sustainer and governor. The concept of Divine Decree (Qadar) is central to understanding Allah's role in maintaining the cosmic order. Qadar refers to Allah's pre-ordained knowledge and plan for all that exists, including the events that occur in the world. This does not imply that human beings are merely puppets controlled by Allah, but rather that Allah has given them free will and the ability to choose between right and wrong. While Allah knows the choices that individuals will make, He does not force them to act in a particular way.

The Quran emphasizes the importance of believing in Qadar, as it acknowledges Allah's absolute knowledge and control over all things. However, it also stresses the importance of human effort and responsibility. Muslims are encouraged to strive for good, to make righteous choices, and to work towards positive change in the world. The belief in Qadar provides comfort and solace in times of difficulty, reminding believers that everything happens according to Allah's wisdom and plan. It also encourages humility, as it acknowledges that all success and blessings ultimately come from Allah.

The concept of 'Tawakkul' (reliance on Allah) is closely related to Qadar. Tawakkul means trusting in Allah's plan and relying on His help, while also taking appropriate action and making necessary efforts. It is a balance between faith and action, recognizing that Allah is the ultimate source of all outcomes. The cosmic order, therefore, is a reflection of Allah's divine wisdom, power, and justice. While human beings have a role to play in shaping their own lives and the world around them, they ultimately submit to Allah's will and trust in His plan.

description: Allah (Arabic: الله) is the Arabic word for God in Islam, representing the one and only deity in Islamic theology. The concept of Allah in Islam is characterized by Tawhid (absolute monotheism) - the belief in the oneness, uniqueness, and indivisibility of God. Allah is described through the 99 Beautiful Names (al-Asma al-Husna), each reflecting a divine attribute: Ar-Rahman (The Most Compassionate), Ar-Rahim (The Most Merciful), Al-Malik (The King), Al-Quddus (The Holy), and others. Islamic theology emphasizes that Allah is transcendent yet immanent, beyond human comprehension yet closer to humanity than their jugular vein (Quran 50:16). Allah is the creator and sustainer of all existence, the source of all revelation (including the Torah, Psalms, Gospel, and Quran), and the ultimate judge on the Day of Resurrection. Muslims express devotion through five daily prayers (Salat), fasting, charity, and pilgrimage, all directed toward Allah alone. The concept of shirk (associating partners with Allah) is considered the gravest sin in Islam.

keyMyths.title: The Creation of Adam
keyMyths.description: Allah created Adam, the first human, from clay and breathed life into him. The angels were commanded to prostrate before Adam, but Iblis (Satan) refused out of pride, leading to his expulsion from Paradise and becoming a symbol of disobedience.

keyMyths.title: The Night Journey (Isra and Mi'raj)
keyMyths.description: The Prophet Muhammad's miraculous night journey from Mecca to Jerusalem (Isra) and his ascension to the heavens (Mi'raj) in the company of the angel Gabriel. During this journey, he met prophets and received instructions from Allah, including the obligation of the five daily prayers.

keyMyths.title: The Story of Prophet Ibrahim and the Sacrifice
keyMyths.description: Allah commanded Prophet Ibrahim (Abraham) to sacrifice his son, Ismail (Ishmael), as a test of faith. Just as Ibrahim was about to carry out the command, Allah intervened and replaced Ismail with a ram, demonstrating Ibrahim's unwavering devotion and establishing the tradition of Eid al-Adha.

keyMyths.title: The Great Flood of Prophet Nuh (Noah)
keyMyths.description: The story of Prophet Nuh (Noah) who was commanded by Allah to build an ark to save himself, his family, and pairs of animals from a devastating flood sent as punishment for the wickedness of humanity. After the flood subsided, Nuh and his companions repopulated the earth.
keyMyths.source: Quran (various verses in Surah Hud and other Surahs)

keyMyths.title: The Angels Harut and Marut
keyMyths.description: Two angels, Harut and Marut, were sent to Earth to test humanity but succumbed to worldly desires and taught magic to the people. This story serves as a cautionary tale about the dangers of temptation and the importance of resisting evil.
keyMyths.source: Quran (2:102) and various interpretations in Islamic literature

worshipPractices.name: Salat (Prayer)
worshipPractices.description: Muslims perform five daily prayers facing the Kaaba in Mecca as commanded by Allah. These prayers involve specific rituals, recitations from the Quran, and are a direct form of communication and submission to Allah's will.

worshipPractices.name: Zakat (Charity)
worshipPractices.description: Giving a portion of one's wealth to the poor and needy is a mandatory act of worship, purifying wealth and fostering social justice. It is seen as fulfilling Allah's command to care for the less fortunate.

festivals.name: Eid al-Fitr
festivals.timing: Marks the end of Ramadan, the month of fasting
festivals.description: A joyous celebration involving special prayers, feasts, and giving to charity. It is a time for gratitude to Allah for the strength to complete the fast and for blessings received.

festivals.name: Eid al-Adha
festivals.timing: Celebrated on the 10th day of Dhu al-Hijjah
festivals.description: Commemorates the willingness of Ibrahim (Abraham) to sacrifice his son as an act of obedience to Allah. Muslims sacrifice an animal and share the meat with family, friends, and the poor, reflecting themes of sacrifice and submission to God.

cultural.modernLegacy: The concept of Allah remains central to Islamic identity and practice worldwide, shaping ethical values, social structures, and artistic expression. Islamic art, literature, and architecture continue to draw inspiration from the divine attributes and teachings associated with Allah, influencing global culture.

cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: Zeus, as the king of the Olympian gods, held supreme power and was considered the ultimate authority in Greek mythology, similar to Allah's supreme and absolute authority in Islam.

While Norse mythology is polytheistic, Odin, as the Allfather, held a central and revered position, embodying wisdom and power, and representing a figure of ultimate leadership, somewhat analogous to Allah's central role in Islam.
Ra, the sun god, was a central deity in ancient Egypt, representing creation, life, and power, reflecting Allah's role as the creator and sustainer in Islam.

associations.name: Tawhid
associations.significance: Tawhid, the concept of the oneness of God, is the foundational principle of Islamic theology.
The 99 names of Allah represent the attributes and qualities of God as explored in Islamic theology.

associations.name: Al-Kursi (The Throne)
associations.significance: Al-Kursi, the Throne of God, symbolizes divine authority and governance, a subject of theological interpretation.

associations.name: Ilm (Knowledge)
associations.significance: Ilm, or knowledge, is a divine attribute and a pathway to understanding God's creation and commandments within Islamic theology.

associations.name: Mizan (Balance)
associations.significance: Mizan, the divine balance or scale, represents justice and accountability, central themes in Islamic theological discussions about judgment.

associations.name: Sidrat al-Muntaha
associations.significance: Sidrat al-Muntaha, the Lote Tree of the Utmost Boundary, represents the limit of knowledge and access to the divine, a concept explored in mystical Islamic theology.

associations.name: Hikmah (Wisdom)
associations.significance: Hikmah, divine wisdom, guides the divine actions and laws, a core element when interpreting theological matters.

companions.name: Jibril (Gabriel)
companions.description: The archangel Jibril is Allah's primary messenger, delivering divine revelations, including the Quran, to prophets. He serves as a crucial link between Allah and humanity.

companions.name: Israfil
companions.description: Israfil is the angel who will blow the trumpet to signal the Day of Judgment. He is a powerful angel tasked with a pivotal role in Allah's divine plan.

companions.name: Mika'il (Michael)
companions.description: Mika'il is an archangel often associated with mercy and providing sustenance to humanity. He carries out Allah's commands related to the natural world and human welfare.

companions.name: Kiraman Katibin
companions.description: These are the noble scribes, angels who record the deeds of every human being. They serve as witnesses for the Day of Judgement and embody Allah's knowledge of all actions.

companions.name: Buraq
companions.description: A mythical steed described as a creature from the heavens that transported prophets, particularly Muhammad, during the Night Journey (Isra and Mi'raj). It is a divine conveyance facilitating Allah's will.

shortDescription: Allah is the singular, all-powerful God in Islam. Revered for absolute oneness (Tawhid), He is the creator, sustainer, and judge of all existence.
subtitle: The One God: Creator, Sustainer, and Ultimate Judge
